pages.text: Comparative ﬂexural behavior of Hybrid Ultra High Performance Fiber Reinforced Concrete with different macro ﬁbers Dong Joo Kim a,⇑, Seung Hun Park a, Gum Sung Ryu b, Kyung Taek Koh b a Department of Civil and Environmental Engineering, Sejong University, 98 Gunja-Dong, Gwangjin-Gu, Seoul 143-747, Republic of Korea b Korea Institute of Construction Technology, 2311 Daewha-Dong ilsan-Gu, Goyang-Si, Gyeonggi-Do 411-712, Republic of Korea article info Article history: Received 24 February 2011 Received in revised form 15 April 2011 Accepted 19 April 2011 Available online 23 May 2011 Keywords: Hybrid Macro ﬁber Micro ﬁber Strain capacity Deﬂection capacity Ultra High Performance Fiber Reinforced Concrete abstract The ﬂexural performance of four Hybrid (H-) Ultra High Performance Fiber Reinforced Concretes (UHPFRCs) with different macro ﬁbers was investigated according to ASTM standards C1018-97 and C 1609/C 1609M-05. Four macro ﬁbers were long smooth (LS-) steel ﬁber, two types of hooked (HA- and HB-) steel ﬁbers, and twisted (T-) steel ﬁbers while one type of micro ﬁber, short smooth (SS-) steel ﬁber, was blended. The enhancements in modulus of rupture, deﬂection capacity and energy absorption capac- ity were different according to the types of macro ﬁber as the amount of micro ﬁber blended increased. The order of ﬂexural performance of H-UHPFRC according to the types of macro ﬁber was as follows: HB- > T- > LS- > HA-ﬁber. The inﬂuence of strain capacity in tension on the ﬂexural performance of H-UHPFRC was also examined. The deﬂection capacity and the ratio between ﬂexural strength and tensile strength were dependent upon the strain capacity of H-UHPFRC. /C2112011 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. 1. Introduction Much research has been conducted to enhance the tensile strength and ductility of Ultra High Performance Fiber Reinforced Concrete (UHPFRC) [24,8,16,7,23,10,11,32]. The advantages of UHPFRC include higher durability, ductility and strength in com- parison with normal concrete and Fiber Reinforced Concrete (FRC) due to its extremely low porosity; dense matrix; high ten- sile/compressive strength; and, ductile tensile behavior. In com- parison with normal steel reinforced concrete, the application of UHPFRC is expected to improve the resistance of buildings and infrastructure under extreme mechanical and environmental loads. However, the cost of 1% volume content of ﬁber applied in UHPFRC is generally higher than that of matrix. Thus, it is impor- tant to minimize the amounts of ﬁber without scarifying the supe- rior performance of UHPFRC. Much interest is now focused on both minimizing the amounts of ﬁber and maximizing the performance of UHPFRC. The tensile or ﬂexural performance of UHPFRC is inﬂuenced by many material parameters. The parameters include strength, stiff- ness, shape, aspect ratio, and Poisson’s ratio of ﬁbers; the strength, stiffness, and shrinkage of matrix; and, the physio-chemical and frictional bond properties at the interface between ﬁber and matrix. Under the assumption that an identical Ultra High Perfor- mance Concrete (UHPC) matrix is used, the type of ﬁber is the most governing factor among the several parameters mentioned above. Thus, the material, geometry, and volume contents of ﬁber should be very carefully determined to improve the ﬂexural performance of UHPFRC. To improve the ﬂexural performance of UHPFRC, one of the promising methods is to blend two or three different ﬁbers to- gether in a matrix

Thus, it is expected that the ductility of FRCC generates much inﬂuence on the ratio between the ﬂexural strength and tensile strength of FRCC. To determine the brittleness or ductility of FRCC, Ward and Li [31] proposed the ratio between ﬂexural strength and tensile strength as a material parameter, i.e., the ratio increases as the ductility of FRCC increases. Later, Lepech and Li [15] carried out an experimental program designed to investigate the inﬂuence of material ductility of Engineered Cementitious Composites (ECC), one of the strain hardening FRCCs, on the ﬂexural behavior of ECC. The ratio between the ﬂexural strength and the tensile strength of ECC was varied between 1.45 and 2.60 according to the different strain capacity of ECC. They concluded that ECC with high ductility (>3.3%) showed very little size effect on the ratio ranging between 2.55 and 2.60. In other words, as the material ductility increases, the ratio between the ﬂexural strength and ten- sile strength of FRCC increases. Soronakom and Mobasher [28] developed a closed form solu- tion of the moment–curvature response of FRC, and applied the model to predict the ﬂexural behavior of FRCC based on the tensile stress–strain response of FRCC. They demonstrated that the ﬂex- ural response of FRCC can be analytically obtained from the tensile and compressive behavior of FRCC. Kim et al. [13] investigated the correlation between tensile and ﬂexural behavior of FRCC and reported that FRCC with higher strain capacity produced higher deﬂection capacity and ﬂexural strength. And, it was found that both the equivalent bending strength and the deﬂection capacity increased as the size of spec- imen decreased. They concluded that the tensile stress–strain re- sponse cannot be uniquely predicted from bending response due to the structural effects from the size of specimen and span length. In other words, similar ﬂexural response could be produced due to any structural effects although the tensile response of FRCC as material properties were quite different, e.g., different tensile strength (ﬁrst and post cracking strength) and strain capacity. The tensile strength of FRCC is closely related with the ﬂexural strength of FRCC. In addition to the tensile strength, the strain capacity of FRCC is also one of most important material parameters inﬂuencing the ﬂexural response of FRCC. By assuming rectangular stress block in compression and per- fectly plastic in tension, ﬂexural strength f MOR can be estimated by using Eq. (2) [18] . fMOR ¼ rpc /C2 31 /C0 c h /C16/C17 1 þ c h ð1 /C0 b1Þ hi ð2Þ where rpc is average uniform tensile stress and is a function of epc, c=h ¼ rpc =ð0:85f 0 c þ rpc Þ, f 0 c is compressive strength, b1 is a factor regarding the depth of compression stress block, c is the depth of neutral axis, and h is the height of specimen. Fig. 6. Flexural test results with high strength steel Hooked B ﬁber (HB-ﬁber) as a macro ﬁber. 4148 D.J. Kim et al. / Construction and Building Materials 25 (2011) 4144–4155

The deﬂection capacity dMOR can be estimated by using Eq. (3) based on the assumption that the moment resistance is maximized when the strain at tensile side reaches strain capacity epc. dMOR ¼ 23/MORL2 216 þ /MORð1 þ mÞL2 6j ðh L Þ2 ð3Þ where /MOR is the curvature at MOR and a function epc /MOR ¼ epc ðh/C0 cÞ /C16/C17 ; m is Poisson’s ratio, j is shape factor for cross section, and L is span length. 5. Experiments The four macro ﬁbers investigated include long smooth steel ﬁber (LS-), two hooked ﬁbers (HA- and HB-), and twisted (T-) steel ﬁbers. The T-ﬁber used has a tri- angular section and six ribs within ﬁber length. The volume content of macro ﬁber is ﬁxed as 1% while the volume contents of micro ﬁber blended are 0.0%, 0.5%, 1.0% and 1.5%. The micro ﬁber used is short smooth (SS-) steel ﬁber: in detail, the length of micro ﬁber is 13 mm and the diameter of ﬁber is 0.2 mm. The composition of the UHPC matrix used and its average compressive strength are given in Table 1 [20,25], while the properties of ﬁbers are given in Table 2. The photos of the LS-, HA-, HB-, T- and SS-ﬁbers are shown in Fig. 2. A universal testing machine used in ﬂexural test is Schmadzu AG-300KNX. The test machine was running in displacement control to obtain ﬂexural load versus deﬂection curve. The speed of displacement applied dur- ing the test was 0.4 mm/min (0.016 in./min). The combination between four macro ﬁbers and four volume contents of micro ﬁber leads sixteen test series as summa- rized in Table 3. For example, the test series blended with 1.0% HA-ﬁber (macro ﬁ- ber) and 1.5% SS-ﬁber (micro ﬁber) in a UHPC matrix is notated as HA10SS15. The specimens with 2% SS-ﬁber (micro ﬁber) were prepared additionally to investigate any favorable synergy in hybrid systems and compare the performance of hybrid system to UHPFRC with one type of ﬁber. 5.1. Materials and specimen preparation In preparing specimens, a Hobart type laboratory mixer with 60 l capacity was used. Cement, silica fume, glass (or silica) powder and sand were ﬁrst dry-mixed for about 10 min. The diameter of sand was lower than 0.5 mm, and the average diam- eter of ﬁller (glass powder) used in the UHPC matrix was about 10 lm. The glass powder was composed of 98% SiO 2, and its density was 2.60 g/cm 3. The water pre-mixed with superplasticizer was then added gradually into the mixture in dry state, and the UHPC matrix was mixed for another 5–10 min. When the state of mortar matrix showed appropriate ﬂow ability for good workability and ade- quate viscosity for uniform ﬁber distribution, micro ﬁbers were ﬁrst added carefully by hand into the mortar mixture and then macro ﬁbers were dispersed. The mortar mixture mixed with both macro and micro ﬁbers was then placed in a mold from one end to the other end of specimen by using a wide scoop with no vibration since the mortar mixture with ﬁbers has self consolidating capacity to ﬁll the mold. Care was taken in maintaining appropriate ﬂow ability and viscosity of mortar matrix to prevent ﬁber gravitation due to the higher density of steel ﬁber than that of the mortar mixture during mixing and placing of the fresh mixture. The amount of superplasticizer was pre-determined to obtain adequate viscosity of matrix for uni- form ﬁber distribution. Specimens cast were covered with plastic sheets and stored at room temperature for 48 h prior to demolding. After demolding specimens, the specimens were water cured with high temperature (90 ± 2 /C176C) for 3 days.

Recently, the inﬂuence of macro ﬁbers on the tensile behavior of H-UHPFRC has been investigated by authors [21]. As the amount of micro ﬁber blended increases, the improvement of the post crack- ing strength, strain capacity and multiple cracking behaviors is very different according to the types of macro ﬁber. Since the ﬂexural behavior of Fiber Reinforced Cementitious Composites [FRCC] is dependent on the tensile stress–strain re- sponse of FRCC, the maximum equivalent bending strength, deﬂec- tion capacity and energy absorption capacity of H-UHPFRC would be highly inﬂuenced by both strain capacity (material ductility) and post cracking strength (material strength) of H-UHPFRC [19,13]. The objective of this research is to investigate the ﬂexural per- formance of H-UHPFRC according to different types of macro ﬁber. The detailed objectives are: (1) to investigate the inﬂuence of dif- ferent macro ﬁbers on the ﬂexural behavior of H-UHPFRC, (2) to investigate the inﬂuence of the micro ﬁber volume contents on the ﬂexural behavior of Hybrid UHPFRC, and (3) to correlate the tensile response (material ductility) and ﬂexural response (struc- tural performance) of H-UHPFRC. 2. Research signiﬁcance Very little information is available about the inﬂuence of the types of macro ﬁber on the ﬂexural behavior of hybrid UHPFRC (H-UHPFRC). This research provides useful experimental data on the inﬂuence of different macro ﬁbers blended with micro ﬁbers on the ﬂexural performance of H-UHPFRC to optimize ﬁber blend- ing in hybrid systems. Furthermore, this research highlights the Fig. 1. Typical load–deﬂection response curves of FRCC. Table 1 Composition of matrix mixtures by weight ratio and compressive strength in Park et al. importance of material ductility (strain capacity) as well as mate- rial strength on the structural (ﬂexural) performance of H-UHPFRC. 3. Flexural parameters of FRCC The ﬂexural behavior of FRCC is categorized into deﬂection hardening and softening behaviors, according to the change of load carrying capacity after ﬁrst cracking as shown in Fig. 1 . Several parameters describing the ﬂexural behavior of FRCC are used in this study to compare the ﬂexural performance of H-UHPFRC according to different macro ﬁbers. The ﬁrst cracking point of FRCC is deﬁned as Limit of Proportionality ( LOP) according to the ASTM C 1018-97 [2]; and, the maximum equivalent bending strength point of FRCC is deﬁned as Modulus of Rupture ( MOR). The detailed dis- cussion about the deﬁnitions of LOP and MOR was provided by Kim et al. [12]. The load value at MOR is notated as PMOR and the corresponding deﬂection (deﬂection capacity) at MOR is notated as dMOR in this study. The equivalent bending strength at MOR, fMOR, can be calcu- lated by using Eq. (1) which was provided by ASTM C 1609/C 1609M-05 [3]. And, the energy equivalent to the area under the load–deﬂection curve up to MOR is notated as ToughMOR. fMOR ¼ PMOR /C1 L bh 2 ð1Þ where L is span length, b is the width of specimen, and, h is the height of specimen. Besides LOP and MOR, three additional points are investigated as follows. L/600: a net deﬂection equal to 1/600 of the span, 0.5 mm (0.02in.) L/150: a net deﬂection equal to 1/150 of the span, 2 mm (0.08in.) L/100: a net deﬂection equal to 1/100 of the span, 3 mm (0.12in.) In the current ASTM standard C 1609, only two points including L/600 and L/150 are recommended to analyze the ﬂexural behavior of FRCC. L/100 point was additionally recommended by Kim et al. [12] since two points including L/600 and L/150 are not enough to describe the ﬂexural behavior of H-UHPFRC showing deﬂection hardening. 4. Correlation between tensile and ﬂexural behavior of FRCC The ﬂexural response of FRCC can be analytically predicted from the tensile and compressive behavior of the material [19,28,29]. Since the ﬂexural behavior of FRCC is strongly correlated with the tensile behavior of FRCC, FRCC with strain hardening behavior in tension surely leads to a deﬂection hardening behavior in ﬂexure [19,13]. And, the ductility of FRCC, in addition to the tensile strength of FRCC, also strongly causes signiﬁcant inﬂuence on the ﬂexural response of FRCC. Fig. 5. Flexural test results with high strength steel Hooked A ﬁber (HA-ﬁber) as a macro ﬁber. D.J. Kim et al. / Construction and Building Materials 25 (2011) 4144–4155 4147

5.2. Test set-up and procedure The geometry of ﬂexural specimen and test set-up is shown in Fig. 3 . The dimension of ﬂexural specimen used was 100 /C2 100 /C2 400 mm (4 /C2 4 /C2 16 in.), and the span length of the specimen was 300 mm (12 in.) according to ASTM stan- dard. Detailed information about the ﬂexural test set-up can be found in ASTM stan- dard and Kim et al. [12]. To minimize the inﬂuence of nonuniform ﬁber distribution in the specimen, the top or bottom surface of the specimen during casting was ro- tated to side when tested although all the specimens tested showed uniform ﬁber distribution. A special steel frame was attached to the ﬂexural specimen to measure net deﬂection of specimen as shown in Fig. 3 . The net deﬂection of ﬂexural speci- men was measured from an LVDT attached to the specimen with the aid of steel frame. The load signal was measured from a load cell directly attached to the bot- tom of the cross head. 5.3. Test results and general discussion The ﬂexural responses of all test series are illustrated by the equivalent bending stress–deﬂection curves in Figs. 4–7 . Each equivalent bending stress–deﬂection curve in Figs. 4–7 is averaged from three to ﬁve test results. The averaged curve is obtained by calculating averaged equivalent bending strength as the deﬂection increases.
